title: Views about AI's impact on society in the next 20 years
subtitle: Survey respondents were asked, "Will artificial intelligence help or harm people in the next 20 years?"
caption:
Views about AI's impact on society in the next 20 years

Survey respondents were asked, "Will artificial intelligence help or harm people in the next 20 years?"

This stacked bar chart displays survey responses from 2021 across eleven countries regarding the anticipated impact of artificial intelligence over the next two decades. The data is segmented into five categories: "Mostly help" (blue), "Neither" (green), "No response" (purple), "No opinion or don't know" (tan), and "Mostly harm" (maroon). In Japan and China, the "Mostly help" category dominates, with both nations recording 61% of respondents selecting this option, while the "Mostly harm" segment remains low at 13% for both. Germany follows closely with 60% expecting AI to mostly help, whereas Italy shows a split where 50% anticipate help and 32% anticipate harm. The United Kingdom and Canada display more balanced distributions, with "Mostly help" ranging between 40% and 47%, and "Mostly harm" holding steady at 29% for both. In France, the "Mostly harm" response rises to 36%, surpassing the 40% who believe it will mostly help. The United States and India show an even split between optimism and pessimism, with 35% and 33% respectively expecting help, matched by 35% and 36% expecting harm. Indonesia and Pakistan exhibit the highest levels of uncertainty or negative outlooks; in Pakistan, the "Mostly harm" category reaches a peak of 52%, while the "Mostly help" segment drops to 19%. The "No opinion or don't know" band is particularly wide in Indonesia at 44%, indicating significant uncertainty in that region compared to others where this category generally stays below 30%. The survey included a global total of 120,000–130,000 people aged 15+, with most countries represented by a nationally representative sample of around 1,000 people.
